item.title: Why Do We Grieve? - Robin May
item.description: Grieving is a uniquely human emotion – or is it? Is the apparent attachment of elephants or orcas to the bodies of dead relatives a sign of grief,…

item.title: Why Do We Laugh? - Robin May
item.description: Laughter is an incredibly powerful and yet mysterious emotion. We laugh with delight, but also surprise. We laugh at jokes, but also at…

item.title: Why Do We Fear? - Robin May
item.description: Fear is one of the earliest emotions to have evolved. Most vertebrates – and possibly some invertebrates – show fear when they are threatened. At…

item.title: The Future of Immunity - Robin May
item.description: Our understanding of the human immune system today is vastly different from that of 50 years ago. This knowledge has led to immune-based therapies…

item.title: Do Computers Get Sick? How Humans and Computers Fight Viruses - Robin May & Victoria Baines
item.description: Whether you are human or computer, viruses can ruin your day, so taking steps to avoid them is important. This lecture will ask whether there are…

item.title: Evolution Tomorrow and Beyond - Robin May
item.description: Evolution has led from amoebae to blue whales and from algae to giant redwoods. So what might it do in the future? What species might evolve in the…

item.title: Artificial Selection: How Humans have Shaped Evolution - Robin May
item.description: We often think of evolution as ‘something that happened’ in the past. But of course, evolution is a constant, powerful process and one that is often…

item.title: A 300,000-Year History of Human Evolution - Robin May
item.description: The species we recognise as our own - anatomically modern humans - has existed for only 300,000 years, a blink of an eye in evolutionary terms. And…
